coredata.dc:title: AIE-active luminogen for highly sensitive and selective detection of picric acid in water samples: Pyridyl as an effective recognition group
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yuqing
Zhang, Yuyang
Liu, Xiaojun
Zhang, Qiong
Kong, Lin
Tian, Yupeng
Li, Gang
Zhang, Xuanjun
Yang, Jiaxiang
coredata.dc:description: Detection of nitroaromatic explosives especially picric acid (PA) is considerably worth concern on human safety and homeland security. Herein, we developed an effective probe (APPTPEP) for PA detection in aqueous medium by combination of 1,4-dihydropyrrolo[3,2-b]pyrrole with tetraphenylethylene and pyridine units. The incorporation of tetraphenylethylene unit in the backbone endows the APPTPEP a significant aggregation induced emission (AIE) behavior. Pyridyl group was proved as recognition site for PA and their strong interaction was confirmed by 1H NMR titration and supported by theoretical calculation. Owing to this strong affinity, APPTPEP exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity (limit of detection 31.5 nM) to PA over other nitroaromatic compounds. This research provides new insights into the rational design of AIE-active materials for effective sensing application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 1,4-dihydropyrrolo[3,2-b]pyrrole
Picric acid
Aggregation-induced emission
Pyridyl group

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of APPTPEP toward PA
Effect of acid on sensing performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV-vis absorption (a) and FL spectra (b) of APPTPEP in DMF/water mixtures with different water volume fractions (inset: optical photographs recorded under 365 nm UV irradiation with various fractions of water).
DLS data showing particle sizes of APPTPEP before (a) and after (b) sensing of PA in water.

Fluorescence spectra of APPTPEP at the presence of different analytes (25 mM) (a); Quenching percentage of APPTPEP (10 mM) with analytes (50 mM) in DMF/water (v/v = 1: 99) mixtures before (black) and after (red) the addition of 25 mM PA (b); Fluorescence spectra of APPTPEP in DMF/water (v/v = 1: 99) containing different amounts of PA (c); Corresponding Stern-Volmer plot of PA. Inset: Stern-Volmer plot obtained at lower concentration of PA (d). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
1H NMR spectra (600 MHz) of chemosensors with PA in the mixed solvents of CDCl3 and DMSO‑d 6 (v/v = 4:6). (a) APPTPEP + PA; (b) APPTPEB + PA.
Optimized structures, HOMO-LUMO and HOMO-1-LUMO+2 levels, and the molecular orbit plot of APPTPEP and APPTPEP + PA.
Schematic illustration of the sensing mechanism.

(a) Fluorescence response of APPTPEP toward PA in real samples. (b) Column diagrams of the fluorescence intensity of APPTPEP (10 mM) + metal ions (50 mM) in aqueous media, blue bars represent metal ions solution and red bars represent metal ion solution with PA (25 mM). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Photographs of fluorescence quenching of APPTPEP for detecting different concentrations of PA under 365 nm UV light. From left to right: blank (i) 5.0 × 10−3 M, (ii)1.0 × 10−3 M, (iii) 5.0 × 10−4 M, (iv) 1.0 × 10−4 M, (v) 5.0 × 10−5 M, (vi) 1.0 × 10−5 M.
Comparison of the sensing performance of some reported probes for PA.

head.ce:title: AIE-active luminogen for highly sensitive and selective detection of picric acid in water samples: Pyridyl as an effective recognition group

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Chemistry for Inorganic/Organic Hybrid Functional Materials, Hefei 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Chemistry for Inorganic/Organic Hybrid Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Health Sciences, University of Macau, Macau SAR, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of nitroaromatic explosives especially picric acid (PA) is considerably worth concern on human safety and homeland security. Herein, we developed an effective probe (APPTPEP) for PA detection in aqueous medium by combination of 1,4-dihydropyrrolo[3,2-b]pyrrole with tetraphenylethylene and pyridine units. The incorporation of tetraphenylethylene unit in the backbone endows the APPTPEP a significant aggregation induced emission (AIE) behavior. Pyridyl group was proved as recognition site for PA and their strong interaction was confirmed by 1H NMR titration and supported by theoretical calculation. Owing to this strong affinity, APPTPEP exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity (limit of detection 31.5 nM) to PA over other nitroaromatic compounds. This research provides new insights into the rational design of AIE-active materials for effective sensing application.

Image 1
A novel 1,4-dihydropyrrolo[3,2-b]pyrrole derivative containing tetraphenylthylene and pyridyl group exhibiting aggregation-induced emission and achieving highly sensitive and selective detection of picric acid in water at acidic condition.

ce:section.ce:para: Picric acid (PA) is an important power explosive, which is superior to its well-known counterpart of TNT. It is also widely used in pesticides, leathers, dyes and pharmaceuticals [1–3]. However, a little amount of PA can cause serious pollution to the soil and aquatic systems [4–7]. Rapid and selective detection of PA has been become one of the most pressing issues [8–18]. Compared with the traditional analytical methods such as gas chromatography, Raman spectroscopy, cyclic voltammetry, etc, [19–31] optical detection, especially the fluorescence technique has its advantages such as simple instrumentation, cost-efficiency, rapid and high sensitivity [25,29,32–35].

So far, a mass of fluorescent materials for PA detection have been developed such as the electron-rich organic dyes, conjugated polymers, quantum dots, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) and nanomaterials [36–43]. Although considerable progress has been acquired, there are still some problems for effective detecting PA in real water samples. One problem needs to be resolved is the selective sensing of PA over other nitroaromatic explosives. Another problem is to overcome terrible aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect. The stubborn ACQ effect of luminophores in aquatic systems always results in drastically negative effects on effective sensing. In recent years, a class of organic molecules have been developed that are non-emissive in dilute solutions but emit intense fluorescence in aggregated state. These organic molecules exhibited great potentials as luminescent materials for a variety of applications such as light-emitting devices, bioimaging and sensing [44–49]. In this work, we report our design of AIE-active chemosensor APPTPEP (Scheme 1 ) for ultrasensitive detection of PA in real water samples.

Specific recognizing group is very important to improve selectivity and sensitivity for sensing applications. In our previous work, both PA and DNP were detected by imidazole and triphenylamine modified fluorescent probes M-1 and M-2 [50], which could not detect PA exclusively and the limit of detection (LOD) is high. Amino functionalized fluorescent molecules ABA and BBA could detect PA with low LOD, however, unable to effectively achieve PA detection in aqueous media [51]. With the aim to develop more effective probes for PA and resolve the above problems, in this work, a new chemosensor of APPTPEP containing tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and pyridine units was designed and synthesized by a simple route. TPE group, as a star molecule, has been proved an efficient way to construct new aggregation-induced emission (AIE) luminogens with high solid-state fluorescence efficiency [52,53]. Pyridyl group was proved to be an effective recognition moiety for PA. APPTPEP showed highly sensitive and selective response to PA with very low LOD. Importantly, APPTPEP could exhibit exclusive sensing ability for the rapid detection of PA over other potential competing nitroaromatic explosive in aqueous media.

The chemicals and solvents including 4-aminobenzyl cyanide, pyridine-4-carboxaldehyde, p-toluenesulfonic acid (TsOH), 2,3-butanedione, 4-(1,2,2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde, t-BuOK, CH3OH, glacial acetic acid, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from a commercial supplier and used directly without any further refinement. Data of 1H NMR was obtained by using Bruker Avance 400 MHz and 600 MHz spectrometer operated at 25 °C. FT-IR spectra were collected from using the KBr pellet method on a Nicolet 380. The data of ESI-MS spectrum were recorded on LTQ Orbitrap XL mass spectrometer. UV-vis absorption spectra were measured using a TU-1901 spectrometer and Fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Hitachi FL-7000 from Hitachi High Technologies Corporation (Tokyo, Japan).
The chemical structures and synthetic route of the compounds are shown in Scheme 1. The detailed procedure is listed as follows.

p-Toluenesulfonic acid (161 mg, 0.93 mmol), 4-aminobenzyl cyanide (1.23 g, 9.3 mmol) and pyridine-4-carboxaldehyde (1.00 g, 9.3 mmol) were dissolved in 4 mL glacial acetic acid and the mixture was heated to 90 °C for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, then 2,3-butanedione (402 mg, 4.7 mmol) were added into the mixture, the resulting mixture was stirred at 90 °C for 3 h. Then, the reaction was cooled to ambient temperature. The crude product was separated by silica gel column chromatography using CH2Cl2/CH3OH (v/v = 15: 1) as eluent to give yellow solid APPP (500 mg, 1.02 mmol). Yield 22%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 4.14 (s, 2H), 6.81 (s, 1H), 7.15 (d, J = 7.50 Hz, 2H), 7.37 (d, J = 8.04 Hz, 2H), 7.48 (d, J = 8.12 Hz, 2H), 8.41 (d, J = 7.50 Hz, 2H). FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3115, 3026, 2250, 1588, 1514, 1460, 1382, 1145, 985, 802.

The parent APPP (100 mg, 0.2 mmol), 4-(1, 2, 2-triphenylvinyl)benzaldehyde (154 mg, 0.45 mmol) and t-BuOK (114 mg, 1 mmol) were dissolved in 6 mL EtOH and the mixture were heated to reflux for 4 h. The reaction was cooled to ambient temperature and poured into 10 mL EtOH, then the precipitated product was obtained by the way of filtration. Finally, the pure product was acquired by several times washing with EtOH. Yellow-green solid APPTPEP (95 mg, 0.08 mmol), yield 39%. 1H NMR (600 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 6.60 (s, 1H), 7.04–7.15 (m, 17H), 7.35 (d, J = 8.40 Hz, 2H), 7.44 (s, 1H), 7.67 (d, J = 8.16 Hz, 2H), 7.71 (d, J = 8.40 Hz, 2H), 8.46 (d, J = 5.52 Hz, 2H), 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ (ppm): 149.83, 146.88, 143.28, 143.13, 142.55, 142.30, 140.14, 139.97, 139.66, 134.35, 133.26, 133.12, 132.00, 131.34, 131.32, 131.26, 128.84, 127.91, 127.88, 127.69, 127.10, 126.76, 125.62, 121.80, 117.90, 109.47, 97.11. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3029, 3019, 2209, 1591, 1510, 1447, 1380, 1143, 851. HRMS (ESI+): m/z = 1175.4822 ([M+H]+), calcd for C86H60N6 + = 1175.4723 (M+).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of APPTPEP toward PA
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent AIE behavior and water dispersibility of the particles promoted us to explore their sensing applications in water media. The selectivity of APPTPEP were firstly quantified by monitoring the fluorescence intensities in the presence of PA, 2, 4-dinitrophenol (DNP), trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2, 4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), nitrotoluene (NT), o-dinitrophenol (o-NP), nitrobenzene (NB) and p-dinitrophenol (p-NP). APPTPEP exhibited strong emission in DMF/water (v/v = 1:99). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the experimental results revealed that PA showed significant quenching of fluorescence. Whereas, fluorescence intensity of other nitroaromatic species showed little change, which suggested the selective sensing behavior of APPTPEP towards PA. Meanwhile, it is worth note that further addition of 2.5 equivalents PA into solution containing other nitroaromatic species led to remarkable quenching of fluorescence (Fig. 3b), indicating that other nitroaromatic species have little interfering effects on the detection of PA by APPTPEP. The above results distinctly exhibited the excellent selectivity of APPTPEP for PA in aqueous media.

Titration experiments were conducted to evaluate the sensitivity of APPTPEP towards PA. As shown in Fig. 3c, when PA was added in solution, significant emission quenching was observed. When 5 equivalents of PA were added, the FL intensity became minuscule and the quenching efficiency is about 99%. The plot of fluorescence intensity at 527 nm versus different PA concentration is shown in Fig. 3c and a linear relation is attained in the range of 0.5–2.5 (Fig. 3d). The quenching results could be quantitatively analyzed with the Stern-Volmer (S-V) equation [59], the equation:
I0/I = K SV [A] + 1
Where I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of analytes, I is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of analytes, [A] is the molar concentration of analytes, and K SV is the quenching constant in M−1. S-V plot showed the bending upward curves, indicating that the fluorescence quenching becomes more efficient with increasing concentration of PA [60–62]. The Ksv constant was calculated and counted to be 2.30 × 106 M−1 (Fig. 3d).

Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching titration was performed at very low concentrations of PA. The limit of detection (LOD) is an important parameter to estimate the sensitivity of probes [63,64], which can be calculated by using:
LOD = 3 δ/K
Where δ is the standard deviation of the initial emission intensity in the absence of the analyte and K is the slope of the linear calibration curve. The plot of the change in FL intensity versus PA concentration results in a linear curve with slope K = 2.83 × 109 (Fig. S5). The LOD of APPTPEP for PA is counted to be 3.15 × 10−8 M (31.5 nM), which signifying its feasibility to detect PA even at ultra-low concentrations.

For comparison, the performance of representative PA fluorescent chemosensors were summarized in Table 1 emphasizing the selectivity, sensitivity, Ksv and LOD. Compared with the reported chemosensors, APPTPEP displayed many attractive features such as brilliant selectivity, superior sensitivity, and detection in water medium.

To gain a better understanding of the interactions between the APPTPEP and PA, 1H NMR titration experiment was conducted in the solvent of CDCl3 and DMSO‑d 6 (v/v = 4:6). As shown in Fig. 4 , H1 and H2 came from the pyridyl group of APPTPEP. With the addition of PA, these proton signals in 1H NMR spectra dramatically shifted to downfield and the Δδ values of H1, H2, H6, H4 and H5 were 0.30, 0.20, 0.12, 0.19 and 0.19, respectively. These results indicated the strong interaction between the pyridyl group with PA [69–71]. No further changes occurred upon addition of excess PA, indicating that this interaction is specific with a molar ratio of 1: 2. Job's plot curve is also agreement it (Fig. S8).

To further verify this specific interaction, another AIE-active molecule APPTPEB was synthesized, where phenyl ring was used to replace pyridyl group of APPTPEP. The bright fluorescence of APPTPEB in water showed negligible change upon addition of PA (Fig. S6). 1H NMR titration experiment was also conducted to evaluate the interaction between APPTPEB and PA. As shown in Fig. 4b, no proton shift was observed after addition of PA (1–4 equivalent), which indicated the low affinity between APPTPEB and PA.

Additionally, the electron density changes were also investigated to understand the sensing mechanism. As shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 5 , the LUMO level (−2.41 eV) of APPTPEP is higher than that of PA (−3.90 eV), which suggested that the possibility of electron transfer from the LUMO of the chemosensor to the lower LUMO of PA. For free APPTPEP, it is electron rich and the electron density of LUMO is mainly distributed at the cyanostilbene moiety. However, upon the addition of PA, the electron density of the LUMO transfer from the cyanostilbene moiety to the pyridyl group of chemosensor because of the electron deficiency of PA, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. These results are well in agreement with the fluorescence responses of APPTPEP to PA. Moreover, the energy gap (ΔE) between HOMO-1 and LUMO+2 of APPTPEP-PA complex (1.45 eV) is smaller than that of APPTPEP (2.71 eV) between HOMO and LUMO, indicating that the APPTPEP-PA complex is more stable than APPTPEP [62,72].

Based on the results above, the detection mechanism of PA was proposed in Fig. 6 . The aggregated APPTPEP shows bright fluorescence in water. PA binds to the pyridyl group of APPTPEP to quench fluorescence by photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. DLS results (Fig. 2b) showed that the particle sizes didn't show obvious change before and after sensing PA, indicative of good stability of APPTPEP aggregates in the sensing process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acid on sensing performance
ce:section.ce:para: Real samples of polluted water are usually acidic. To further evaluate the effect of acid on sensing performance, experiments were conducted at the presence of trifluoroacetic acid (TFA). As shown in Fig. S8, the isobestic point was attained at 437 nm, suggesting a reactive equilibrium of the two components (Fig. S9a). Also, it is observed that the fluorescence intensity of APPTPEP decreased not obviously with increasing trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) concentration (Fig. S9b). Therefore, APPTPEP is highly promising for sensing PA in water at acidic condition.

Owing to the excellent sensing performance and water dispersibility, we then applied APPTPEP for practical sensing PA in real water samples. We collected the water samples including distilled water, tap water, pond water, Feicui lake (a lake in Anhui Province, China) water. It was found that the fluorescence response for these samples followed the same pattern with the real samples (Fig. 7 a). Also, different metal ions were also measured because of their existence in real samples (Fig. 7b). Experimental results showed no obvious influence on the present system, suggesting the great potentials for monitoring PA in real water samples.

For practical use, we also prepared test strips by placing Whatman filter paper into CH2Cl2 solution of APPTPEP for 5 min and then subsequently drying in air. Various concentrations of PA were checked on the strips by applying small spots of different concentrations of PA (PA was dissolved in EtOH). The test strips exhibited green fluorescence without PA, undergo perceptible fluorescence change from green emission to non-emission upon illumination with a hand-held UV lamp (Fig. 8 ). The results suggested that are efficient fluorescent probes for conveniently detecting PA.

Caution! PA, TNT and other nitroaromatic compounds have highly explosive characters. They should be used with the best safety precautions and extreme care. Only small quantities can be handled in the testing process.

In summary, we have designed and synthesized an AIE-active probe APPTPEP for effective sensing of PA in aqueous medium with high selectivity, high sensitivity (limit of detection 31.5 nM), and fast response to PA. Pyridyl group was proved to act as specific binding moiety responsible for effective sensing. The strong interaction between PA and pyridyl moiety was confirmed by 1H NMR titration experiments, which was also supported by theoretical calculation. APPTPEP showed excellent sensing performance in acidic condition and at the presence of different metal ions, which enabled it as a very promising fluorescent probe for monitoring pollution in real water samples. The combination of AIE fluorophores with recognizing group was proved as a very effective strategy for rational design of chemosensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51673001, 51432001), the Educational Commission of Anhui Province of China (KJ2014ZD02), and the Macao Science and Technology Development Fund (Grant No.: 052/2015/A2 and 082/2016/A2) and Research Grant of the University of Macau (Grant Nos. MYRG2016-00058-FHS and MYRG2017-00066-FHS).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.11.034.
